sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Gregory, presents with a two-and-a-half-week history of right knee pain on the outside of the knee, which he attributes to a twisting injury while stepping off a curb.
He reports that the pain started after the initial ankle soreness from the injury resolved, and is now accompanied by a clicking sensation when walking.
The pain is exacerbated by standing on one foot for an extended period, leaning against a wall, and by full squats.
He denies pain at rest, but notes that the knee clicks when it's resting on his heel.
He has not taken any pain medication other than ibuprofen, which provides some relief at night.
He is able to perform activities such as riding a bike, but experiences clicking and pain with full squats.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of breaking his right second toe three times over the past 10 years, which is associated with numbness and tingling in the foot, but this is not related to the current knee injury.
Review of systems: The patient denies any numbness or tingling in his foot, other than the pre-existing condition related to the toe breaks.
Current medications: Ibuprofen, but he only takes it at night and not during the day due to his work schedule.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has a slight edema and a slight effusion on the right knee.
There is no pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
The patient has a positive varus test, but a full range of motion.
The patient has a negative Lachman sign.
The patient has a palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulse.
Test Results: The patient's knee x-ray does not show any acute fracture or bony abnormality.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I diagnose the patient with a lateral collateral ligament strain of the right knee.
Problem list: Right knee lateral collateral ligament strain; suspected.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once a day for pain management.
I refer the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the muscles around the injured area and prevent future injuries.
I instruct the patient to continue icing the knee at least four times a day, 20 minutes on and 20 minutes off, to help manage pain and inflammation.
Follow-up: I schedule a follow-up appointment to review the patient's progress and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
